Brewing the Perfect Cup: A Beginner's Guide
Temperature, steep time, and water quality — the three things that matter most when brewing loose-leaf tea at home.

Brewing loose-leaf tea at home can feel intimidating at first, but the fundamentals are simple. Once you understand a few key variables, you'll be pulling incredible cups every time.

## Water Temperature

This is the one most people get wrong. Boiling water is too hot for delicate teas and will turn them bitter. Here's a quick guide:

- **Green tea**: 70–80°C (160–175°F)
- **White tea**: 75–85°C (165–185°F)
- **Oolong**: 85–95°C (185–205°F)
- **Black tea**: 95–100°C (203–212°F)
- **Herbal tisanes**: Full boil, 100°C (212°F)

If you don't have a temperature-controlled kettle, boil water and let it sit for 1–2 minutes before pouring over green or white teas.

## Steep Time

Over-steeping is just as damaging as the wrong temperature. Start at the low end and adjust to your taste:

- **Green tea**: 1–3 minutes
- **White tea**: 2–4 minutes
- **Oolong**: 2–5 minutes (varies a lot by style)
- **Black tea**: 3–5 minutes
- **Herbal tisanes**: 5–7 minutes

## Water Quality

Tea is mostly water, so water quality matters more than people realise. Filtered water makes a noticeable difference — the minerals and chlorine in tap water can muddy the flavour. If you're in a hard-water area, this single change will transform your brews.

## The Golden Rule

The best cup of tea is the one you enjoy. Use these guidelines as a starting point, then experiment. More leaves for a stronger brew, shorter steeps for something lighter. Your palate is the final judge.

Stop in and ask us — we love talking through brewing specifics for any tea on our menu.

Long before it became a coffee-shop staple, chai was a bold, spiced brew with deep roots in South Asian culture. Here's the real story.

Walk into any café today and you'll see "chai latte" on the board. But the drink most people know is a fairly distant cousin of the original. Let's go back to the real thing.

## What Chai Actually Means

In Hindi and Urdu, *chai* simply means "tea." The spiced version — the one with cardamom, ginger, cinnamon, and clove — is called *masala chai* ("spiced tea"). When you order a "chai tea latte," you're technically asking for a "tea tea latte." Worth knowing.

## A History of the Cup

Tea came to India through British colonial trade in the 1800s, but the masala chai we know today evolved gradually through the 20th century. Street vendors called *chaiwalas* began selling spiced, milky tea as an affordable, warming drink. Each region developed its own variation — more ginger in some areas, more cardamom in others, sometimes black pepper, sometimes fennel.

The drink was never standardised, and that's part of its beauty. Every family has a recipe. Every chaiwala has a secret.

## Our Spiced Chai

Our house Spiced Chai blend draws from that tradition: bold Assam black tea, green cardamom, fresh ginger, cinnamon bark, and whole cloves. We keep the spice mix loose and generous — because a timid chai is a sad chai.

Ask for it with steamed milk and a small spoon of honey. That's how we love it.

First flush Darjeeling and fresh-picked Japanese Shincha are in the shop. Here's what makes these early harvest teas so special.

Spring is our favourite time of year at the shop. The first harvests of the season arrive — bright, fresh, and full of life — and we get to share them with you.

## First Flush Darjeeling

Picked in March and April from the high-elevation gardens of the Darjeeling region, first flush teas are the most prized of the year. The leaves are small and delicate, the liquor a pale amber-gold, and the flavour unlike anything else — floral, lively, with that distinctive muscatel character that Darjeeling is famous for.

First flush is best drunk without milk. Brew it light (around 85°C, 2–3 minutes) and savour it slowly.

## Shincha: Japan's New Tea

*Shincha* literally means "new tea" in Japanese — it refers to the very first green tea harvest of the year, picked before the main sencha season begins. It has a soft sweetness and a freshness that doesn't last long after harvest, which is part of what makes it so special.

We only receive a small amount of Shincha each year, so when it's gone, it's gone until next spring.

## Come Try Them

Both teas are available by the cup this week. Come in, and we'll brew you a tasting comparison if you'd like to experience the difference between spring and autumn harvest side by side. It's one of those moments that makes tea nerds of people.

See you soon.

See you soon.
